name: Dense Models
expansion: Dense Model (all parameters active per token)
oneliner: 100% of parameters active for every token — the standard transformer design. Simpler than MoE but compute scales linearly with param count.
explanation: Dense models are the standard transformer architecture where every parameter is active for every token, meaning all attention heads and feed-forward layers participate in each computation. The term became necessary to distinguish from Mixture-of-Experts and other sparse architectures. Dense models are simpler to train, serve, and fine-tune, with no routing instabilities or load-balancing concerns. They are preferred when memory rather than compute is the bottleneck, when infrastructure simplicity matters, or when deploying on a single GPU.
fundamentals: Per-token FLOPS $\approx$ 2 $\times$ total_params (rough approximation). 70B dense model: ~140 TFLOPS/token. Same capacity as MoE ~280B total/70B active — same compute, 4$\times$ less memory. When model card lists single param count without total/active distinction → dense. Examples: LLaMA 3 8B/70B/405B, Mistral 7B, Qwen 2.5 72B, Gemma 31B.
sources: Vaswani et al., 'Attention Is All You Need,' 2017
Hoffmann et al., 'Training Compute-Optimal Large Language Models (Chinchilla),' arXiv:2203.15556
